Across seven independent substrates, we show that hub persistence and rank divergence under the Functional Proximity Law recover operational organization that domain experts describe as logic: axiomatic load-bearing structure in formal mathematics, control and contract structure in legacy software, conserved hub grammar across approx. 600 million years of neural evolution, catalytic role organization in a published prebiotic autocatalytic network, carry-path dominance in a 4-bit digital circuit, betweenness persistence in the ISCAS85 c432 standard benchmark (n=196), and a directional formal-systems replication in the Coq Corelib (n=17). A key methodological finding: degree-based hub persistence is weak between physical wiring and simulation state-correlation layers (r=0.21 in c432), while betweenness-based persistence is stronger (r=0.77 in the 4-bit ALU post-hoc; r=0.34 in c432). The ISCAS85 pre-registered primary hypothesis was CONFIRMED (degree r=0.426, p=0.002, Spearman r=0.551). The formal-systems claim is supported by two proof-assistant corpora: Lean 4 mathlib4 (CONFIRMED, r=0.777, p=0.004) and Coq Corelib (PARTIAL, direction confirmed, r=0.288, p=0.287, n=17, underpowered). All seven experiments were pre-registered before analysis.

AI.tldr: A pre-registered study across 7 domains (math, software, neural networks, prebiotic chemistry, digital circuits) shows that functional load-bearing organization can be detected through multilayer network analysis, particularly via hub persistence and betweenness-based measures, with formal validation in proof-assistant corpora.
AI.motivation: To test whether dependency topology reveals functional load-bearing organization as a measurable structural property (not metaphor), recoverable via multilayer network analysis across diverse substrates.
AI.method: Multilayer network analysis measuring hub persistence and rank divergence under the Functional Proximity Law across 7 independent substrates, with pre-registered hypotheses tested on ISCAS85 benchmark, Lean 4 mathlib4, and Coq Corelib.
AI.result: Degree-based hub persistence is weak (r=0.21 in c432); betweenness persistence is stronger (r=0.77 in 4-bit ALU, r=0.34 in c432). ISCAS85 hypothesis CONFIRMED (degree r=0.426, p=0.002; Spearman r=0.551). Lean 4 mathlib4 CONFIRMED (r=0.777, p=0.004). Coq Corelib PARTIAL (r=0.288, p=0.287, underpowered n=17).
AI.conclusion: The Functional Proximity Law reliably recovers operational/logical organization across diverse substrates; betweenness centrality outperforms degree in cross-layer persistence; formal proof corpora provide validation, though Coq results require larger samples.

title: Structure-Informed Multiple Sequence Alignment: A Formal Model and Hardness Results
summary:
We formulate a structure-informed multiple sequence alignment problem, denoted MSA-S. The model abstracts biological sequences as strings and structural information as designated position-pairs. It augments a fixed pairwise string score, defined by a fixed non-gap symbol-pair scoring rule and fixed affine gap penalties, with a binary overlap score on designated position-pairs, which can be interpreted as a contact-map overlap score in structural applications. This yields a fixed-score, integer-valued optimization model suitable for complexity-theoretic analysis.
  Under this formulation, we show that the decision problem MSA-S-DEC is NP-complete for a broad class of fixed pairwise string scoring schemes. We also show that NP-hardness persists even under the restriction that every designated position-pair set is nonempty and the pair-overlap threshold is strictly positive. For the associated scalarized optimization problem MSA-S-OPT(lambda) with any fixed rational constant lambda >= 1, we further show that, under the canonical unit scheme for the non-gap symbol-pair scoring rule, MSA-S-OPT(lambda) admits no polynomial-time approximation scheme (PTAS) even for two input strings (k = 2), unless P = NP.
  These results establish a formal complexity-theoretic baseline for structure-informed multiple sequence alignment.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that structure-informed multiple sequence alignment (MSA-S) is NP-complete and has no PTAS even for two strings under certain conditions.
AI.motivation: To establish a formal complexity-theoretic baseline for structure-informed multiple sequence alignment, which incorporates structural information (contact-map overlap) into traditional string-based alignment.
AI.method: The authors formulate MSA-S as an optimization model combining pairwise string scores with binary overlap scores on designated position-pairs, then prove NP-completeness of the decision problem MSA-S-DEC and inapproximability of MSA-S-OPT(lambda) via complexity-theoretic reductions.
AI.result: MSA-S-DEC is NP-complete for a broad class of scoring schemes; NP-hardness holds even with nonempty position-pair sets and positive thresholds; MSA-S-OPT(lambda) has no PTAS even for k=2 (two strings) unless P=NP.
AI.conclusion: Structure-informed MSA is computationally hard (NP-complete, no PTAS), establishing a formal complexity baseline for future algorithmic approaches.
